channel.title: Kyunolab Mystery Archive
channel.description: Legends, folklore, mysteries, and strange tales.

item.title: Rabbit's Foot Luck Origin: The Charm, the Animal, and the Superstition of Carried Luck
item.description: Rabbit's foot luck is a folk belief in which a preserved rabbit's foot is carried as a charm for good fortune or protection.

item.title: Pandora's Box Origin: The Jar, the Box, and the Mistake That Changed a Mythic Object
item.description: Pandora's Box is the familiar modern name for a Greek mythic object that older tradition more accurately describes as a jar or pithos.

item.title: Lake Titicaca Legendary Place: Sacred Water, Origin Stories, and Andean Memory
item.description: Lake Titicaca is a real Andean lake strongly associated with sacred geography, origin stories, and Inca memory.

item.title: St. Elmo's Fire Phenomenon: Blue Light, Storms, and the Folklore of Sailors
item.description: St. Elmo's Fire is a luminous electrical phenomenon that can appear as a blue or violet glow around pointed objects during storm conditions.

item.title: Hy-Brasil Legendary Island: The Vanishing Land on Old Atlantic Maps
item.description: Hy-Brasil is a legendary island often placed west of Ireland on old maps and described as difficult or impossible to reach.

item.title: Cerberus Mythic Creature: The Three-Headed Dog at the Gate of the Underworld
item.description: Cerberus is the Greek mythic guard dog of the underworld, commonly remembered as having three heads and standing at the boundary of Hades.

item.title: Sisyphus Myth: The Stone, the Hill, and the Punishment That Never Ends
item.description: Sisyphus is a Greek mythic figure condemned in the underworld to roll a stone uphill, only for it to fall back again.

item.title: Goatman Modern Legend: Bridges, Backroads, and the Half-Human Figure
item.description: Goatman is a modern monster legend about a half-human, half-goat figure often connected with bridges, wooded roads, and local warnings.

item.title: Headless Horseman Legend: Night Roads, Missing Heads, and the Rider in Folklore
item.description: The Headless Horseman is a ghostly rider figure remembered through Sleepy Hollow and older folklore about headless apparitions on roads or battle-haunted places.

item.title: Flying Dutchman Folklore: The Ghost Ship, the Horizon, and a Maritime Legend
item.description: The Flying Dutchman is a maritime legend about a ghost ship condemned to sail the seas and appear on the horizon.

item.title: Tower of London Ghosts: Royal Memory, Execution Stories, and a Haunted Landmark
item.description: Tower of London ghost stories attach haunting traditions to a real historic landmark associated with imprisonment, executions, royal power, and public memory.

item.title: Jeff the Killer Creepypasta: The Smile, the Image, and an Internet Horror Legend
item.description: Jeff the Killer is a creepypasta figure known for a pale face, carved-looking smile, and the phrase go to sleep.

item.title: The Licked Hand Urban Legend: The Dog, the Bed, and the Message on the Wall
item.description: The Licked Hand is a modern urban legend about someone alone at night who feels a reassuring lick from what they believe is the family dog.

item.title: Evil Eye Origin: How a Harmful Glance Became One of the World's Oldest Protective Beliefs
item.description: The evil eye is the belief that a harmful or envious glance can bring misfortune. Many cultures developed charms, gestures, or sayings to protect against it.

item.title: Sword in the Stone Legend: The Arthurian Object That Proved a King
item.description: The Sword in the Stone is an Arthurian legend in which the rightful king is revealed by pulling a sword from stone or anvil. The act proves legitimacy through an impossible test.

item.title: Mount Kailash Legendary Place: The Sacred Mountain People Circle But Do Not Climb
item.description: Mount Kailash is a sacred mountain in Tibet revered in several religious traditions. It is known for pilgrimage circuits around the mountain rather than ordinary summit climbing.

item.title: Raining Animals Phenomenon: When Fish, Frogs, and Weather Become Folklore
item.description: Raining animals refers to reports of fish, frogs, or other small creatures falling from the sky. Possible explanations often involve waterspouts or strong weather lifting animals from nearby water.

item.title: Lyonesse Lost Land Legend: The Drowned Kingdom at the Edge of Arthurian Memory
item.description: Lyonesse is a legendary lost land often placed off the coast of Cornwall. Later tradition connects it with drowned kingdoms, coastal memory, and Arthurian material.

item.title: Griffin Folklore: The Eagle-Lion Guardian of Treasure and Power
item.description: The griffin is a mythic creature usually combining the head and wings of an eagle with the body of a lion. It is often linked with guardianship, treasure, and powerful places.

item.title: Persephone and the Underworld: The Greek Myth Behind Loss, Return, and the Seasons
item.description: The Persephone myth tells how Persephone is taken to the underworld, how Demeter grieves, and how Persephone returns for part of the year. The cycle is often connected with seasonal change.
